_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities from one month to thirty years; a normal upward slope indicates longer-term yields exceed shorter-term yields, reflecting economic growth expectations. An inverted yield curve occurs when short-term yields exceed long-term yields, historically preceding every modern US recession by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.48% (June 2026), and the 10-year minus 2-year spread (2s10s) is 0.35 percentage points, indicating a positive slope. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the curve was inverted or at zero, marking its deepest inversion at 0.0 points.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread serves as a key recession indicator; an inversion (negative spread) has preceded all recent US recessions. The data shows the spread was inverted throughout 2022-2024 but has since re-steepened above zero to 0.35 points, suggesting some market reassessment. The New York Fed recession probability is currently 31%, signaling moderate risk but not certainty. Importantly, the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before recessions, so the recent positive spread does not eliminate recession risk but may reflect the market adjusting its outlook.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve's positive slope after a prolonged inversion indicates a shift away from immediate recession fears, but the elevated NY Fed recession probability and historical context advise caution. The partial steepening signals market recalibration rather than definitive alleviation of recession risk.
